Math401Math 401, Summer 2025: Freiwald research project notesMath 401, Topic 3: Separable Hilbert spaces

Math401 Topic 3: Separable Hilbert spaces

Infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces

Recall from Topic 1.

L2L^2L2 space

Let λ\lambdaλ be a measure on R\mathbb{R}R, or any other field you are interested in.

A function is square integrable if

∫R∣f(x)∣2dλ(x)<∞\int_\mathbb{R} |f(x)|^2 d\lambda(x)<\infty∫R​∣f(x)∣2dλ(x)<∞

L2L^2L2 space and general Hilbert spaces

Definition of L2(R,λ)L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)L2(R,λ)

The space L2(R,λ)L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)L2(R,λ) is the space of all square integrable, measurable functions on R\mathbb{R}R with respect to the measure λ\lambdaλ (The Lebesgue measure).

The Hermitian inner product is defined by

⟨f,g⟩=∫Rf(x)‾g(x)dλ(x)\langle f,g\rangle=\int_\mathbb{R} \overline{f(x)}g(x) d\lambda(x)⟨f,g⟩=∫R​f(x)​g(x)dλ(x)

The norm is defined by

∥f∥=∫R∣f(x)∣2dλ(x)\|f\|=\sqrt{\int_\mathbb{R} |f(x)|^2 d\lambda(x)}∥f∥=∫R​∣f(x)∣2dλ(x)​

The space L2(R,λ)L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)L2(R,λ) is complete.

[Proof ignored here]

Recall the definition of complete metric space .

The inner product space L2(R,λ)L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)L2(R,λ) is complete.

Note that by some general result in point-set topology, a normed vector space can always be enlarged so as to become complete. This process is called completion of the normed space.

Some exercise is showing some hints for this result:

Show that the subspace of L2(R,λ)L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)L2(R,λ) consisting of square integrable continuous functions is not closed.

Suggestion: consider the sequence of continuous functions f1(x),f2(x),⋯f_1(x), f_2(x),\cdotsf1​(x),f2​(x),⋯, where fn(x)f_n(x)fn​(x) is defined by the following graph:

Show that fnf_nfn​ converges in the L2L^2L2 norm to a function f∈L2(R,λ)f\in L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)f∈L2(R,λ) but the limit function fff is not continuous. Draw the graph of fnf_nfn​ to make this clear.

Definition of general Hilbert space

A Hilbert space is a complete inner product vector space.

General Pythagorean theorem

Let u1,u2,⋯ ,uNu_1,u_2,\cdots,u_Nu1​,u2​,⋯,uN​ be an orthonormal set in an inner product space V\mathscr{V}V (may not be complete). Then for all v∈Vv\in \mathscr{V}v∈V,

∥v∥2=∑i=1N∣⟨v,ui⟩∣2+∥v−∑i=1N⟨v,ui⟩ui∥2\|v\|^2=\sum_{i=1}^N |\langle v,u_i\rangle|^2+\left\|v-\sum_{i=1}^N \langle v,u_i\rangle u_i\right\|^2∥v∥2=i=1∑N​∣⟨v,ui​⟩∣2+​v−i=1∑N​⟨v,ui​⟩ui​​2

[Proof ignored here]

Bessel’s inequality

Let u1,u2,⋯ ,uNu_1,u_2,\cdots,u_Nu1​,u2​,⋯,uN​ be an orthonormal set in an inner product space V\mathscr{V}V (may not be complete). Then for all v∈Vv\in \mathscr{V}v∈V,

∑i=1N∣⟨v,ui⟩∣2≤∥v∥2\sum_{i=1}^N |\langle v,u_i\rangle|^2\leq \|v\|^2i=1∑N​∣⟨v,ui​⟩∣2≤∥v∥2

Immediate from the general Pythagorean theorem.

Orthonormal bases

An orthonormal subset SSS of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H is a set all of whose elements have norm 1 and are mutually orthogonal. (∀u,v∈S,⟨u,v⟩=0\forall u,v\in S, \langle u,v\rangle=0∀u,v∈S,⟨u,v⟩=0)

Definition of orthonormal basis

An orthonormal subset of SSS of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H is an orthonormal basis of H\mathscr{H}H if there are no other orthonormal subsets of H\mathscr{H}H that contain SSS as a proper subset.

Theorem of existence of orthonormal basis

Every separable Hilbert space has an orthonormal basis.

[Proof ignored here]

Theorem of Fourier series

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a separable Hilbert space with an orthonormal basis {en}\{e_n\}{en​}. Then for any f∈Hf\in \mathscr{H}f∈H,

f=∑n=1∞⟨f,en⟩enf=\sum_{n=1}^\infty \langle f,e_n\rangle e_nf=n=1∑∞​⟨f,en​⟩en​

The series converges to some g∈Hg\in \mathscr{H}g∈H.

[Proof ignored here]

Fourier series in L2([0,2π],λ)L^2([0,2\pi],\lambda)L2([0,2π],λ)

Let f∈L2([0,2π],λ)f\in L^2([0,2\pi],\lambda)f∈L2([0,2π],λ).

fN(x)=∑n:∣n∣≤Ncneinx2πf_N(x)=\sum_{n:|n|\leq N} c_n\frac{e^{inx}}{\sqrt{2\pi}}fN​(x)=n:∣n∣≤N∑​cn​2π​einx​

where cn=12π∫02πf(x)e−inxdxc_n=\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} f(x)e^{-inx} dxcn​=2π1​∫02π​f(x)e−inxdx.

The series converges to some f∈L2([0,2π],λ)f\in L^2([0,2\pi],\lambda)f∈L2([0,2π],λ) as N→∞N\to \inftyN→∞.

This is the Fourier series of fff.

Hermite polynomials

The subspace spanned by polynomials is dense in L2(R,λ)L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)L2(R,λ).

An orthonormal basis of L2(R,λ)L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)L2(R,λ) can be obtained by the Gram-Schmidt process on {1,x,x2,⋯ }\{1,x,x^2,\cdots\}{1,x,x2,⋯}.

The polynomials are called the Hermite polynomials.

Isomorphism and ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ space

Definition of isomorphic Hilbert spaces

Let H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ be two Hilbert spaces.

H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ are isomorphic if there exists a surjective linear map U:H1→H2U:\mathscr{H}_1\to \mathscr{H}_2U:H1​→H2​ that is bijective and preserves the inner product.

⟨Uf,Ug⟩=⟨f,g⟩\langle Uf,Ug\rangle=\langle f,g\rangle⟨Uf,Ug⟩=⟨f,g⟩

for all f,g∈H1f,g\in \mathscr{H}_1f,g∈H1​.

When H1=H2\mathscr{H}_1=\mathscr{H}_2H1​=H2​, the map UUU is called unitary.

ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ space

The space ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ is the space of all square summable sequences.

ℓ2={(an)n=1∞:∑n=1∞∣an∣2<∞}\ell_2=\left\{(a_n)_{n=1}^\infty: \sum_{n=1}^\infty |a_n|^2<\infty\right\}ℓ2​={(an​)n=1∞​:n=1∑∞​∣an​∣2<∞}

An example of element in ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ is (1,0,0,⋯ )(1,0,0,\cdots)(1,0,0,⋯).

With inner product

⟨(an)n=1∞,(bn)n=1∞⟩=∑n=1∞an‾bn\langle (a_n)_{n=1}^\infty, (b_n)_{n=1}^\infty\rangle=\sum_{n=1}^\infty \overline{a_n}b_n⟨(an​)n=1∞​,(bn​)n=1∞​⟩=n=1∑∞​an​​bn​

It is a Hilbert space (every Cauchy sequence in ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​ converges to some element in ℓ2\ell_2ℓ2​).

Bounded operators and continuity

Let T:V→WT:\mathscr{V}\to \mathscr{W}T:V→W be a linear map between two vector spaces V\mathscr{V}V and W\mathscr{W}W.

We define the norm of ∥⋅∥\|\cdot\|∥⋅∥ on V\mathscr{V}V and W\mathscr{W}W.

Then TTT is continuous if for all u∈Vu\in \mathscr{V}u∈V, if un→uu_n\to uun​→u in V\mathscr{V}V, then T(un)→T(u)T(u_n)\to T(u)T(un​)→T(u) in W\mathscr{W}W.

Using the delta-epsilon language, we can say that TTT is continuous if for all ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, there exists a δ>0\delta>0δ>0 such that if ∥u−v∥<δ\|u-v\|<\delta∥u−v∥<δ, then ∥T(u)−T(v)∥<ϵ\|T(u)-T(v)\|<\epsilon∥T(u)−T(v)∥<ϵ.

Definition of bounded operator

A linear map T:V→WT:\mathscr{V}\to \mathscr{W}T:V→W is bounded if

∥T∥=sup⁡∥u∥=1∥T(u)∥<∞\|T\|=\sup_{\|u\|=1}\|T(u)\|< \infty∥T∥=∥u∥=1sup​∥T(u)∥<∞

Theorem of continuity and boundedness

A linear map T:V→WT:\mathscr{V}\to \mathscr{W}T:V→W is continuous if and only if it is bounded.

[Proof ignored here]

Definition of bounded Hilbert space

The set of all bounded linear operators in V\mathscr{V}V is denoted by B(V)\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{V})B(V).

Direct sum of Hilbert spaces

Suppose H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ are two Hilbert spaces.

The direct sum of H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ is the Hilbert space H1⊕H2\mathscr{H}_1\oplus \mathscr{H}_2H1​⊕H2​ with the inner product

⟨(u1,u2),(v1,v2)⟩=⟨u1,v1⟩H1+⟨u2,v2⟩H2\langle (u_1,u_2),(v_1,v_2)\rangle=\langle u_1,v_1\rangle_{\mathscr{H}_1}+\langle u_2,v_2\rangle_{\mathscr{H}_2}⟨(u1​,u2​),(v1​,v2​)⟩=⟨u1​,v1​⟩H1​​+⟨u2​,v2​⟩H2​​

Such space is denoted by H1⊕H2\mathscr{H}_1\oplus \mathscr{H}_2H1​⊕H2​.

A countable direct sum of Hilbert spaces can be defined similarly, as long as it is bounded.

That is, {un:n=1,2,⋯ }\{u_n:n=1,2,\cdots\}{un​:n=1,2,⋯} is a sequence of elements in Hn\mathscr{H}_nHn​, and ∑n=1∞∥un∥2<∞\sum_{n=1}^\infty \|u_n\|^2<\infty∑n=1∞​∥un​∥2<∞.

The inner product in such countable direct sum is defined by

⟨(un)n=1∞,(vn)n=1∞⟩=∑n=1∞⟨un,vn⟩Hn\langle (u_n)_{n=1}^\infty, (v_n)_{n=1}^\infty\rangle=\sum_{n=1}^\infty \langle u_n,v_n\rangle_{\mathscr{H}_n}⟨(un​)n=1∞​,(vn​)n=1∞​⟩=n=1∑∞​⟨un​,vn​⟩Hn​​

Such space is denoted by H=⨁n=1∞Hn\mathscr{H}=\bigoplus_{n=1}^\infty \mathscr{H}_nH=⨁n=1∞​Hn​.

Closed subspaces of Hilbert spaces

Definition of closed subspace

A subspace M\mathscr{M}M of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H is closed if every convergent sequence in M\mathscr{M}M converges to some element in M\mathscr{M}M.

Definition of pairwise orthogonal subspaces

Two subspaces M1\mathscr{M}_1M1​ and M2\mathscr{M}_2M2​ of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H are pairwise orthogonal if ⟨u,v⟩=0\langle u,v\rangle=0⟨u,v⟩=0 for all u∈M1u\in \mathscr{M}_1u∈M1​ and v∈M2v\in \mathscr{M}_2v∈M2​.

Orthogonal projections

Definition of orthogonal complement

The orthogonal complement of a subspace M\mathscr{M}M of a Hilbert space H\mathscr{H}H is the set of all elements in H\mathscr{H}H that are orthogonal to every element in M\mathscr{M}M.

It is denoted by M⊥={u∈H:⟨u,v⟩=0,∀v∈M}\mathscr{M}^\perp=\{u\in \mathscr{H}: \langle u,v\rangle=0,\forall v\in \mathscr{M}\}M⊥={u∈H:⟨u,v⟩=0,∀v∈M}.

Projection theorem

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a Hilbert space and M\mathscr{M}M be a closed subspace of H\mathscr{H}H. Then for any v∈Hv\in \mathscr{H}v∈H can be uniquely decomposed as v=u+wv=u+wv=u+w where u∈Mu\in \mathscr{M}u∈M and w∈M⊥w\in \mathscr{M}^\perpw∈M⊥.

[Proof ignored here]

Dual Hilbert spaces

Norm of linear functionals

Let H\mathscr{H}H be a Hilbert space.

The norm of a linear functional f∈H∗f\in \mathscr{H}^*f∈H∗ is defined by

∥f∥=sup⁡∥u∥=1∣f(u)∣\|f\|=\sup_{\|u\|=1}|f(u)|∥f∥=∥u∥=1sup​∣f(u)∣

Definition of dual Hilbert space

The dual Hilbert space of H\mathscr{H}H is the space of all bounded linear functionals on H\mathscr{H}H.

It is denoted by H∗\mathscr{H}^*H∗.

H∗=B(H,C)={f:H→C:f is linear and ∥f∥<∞}\mathscr{H}^*=\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H},\mathbb{C})=\{f: \mathscr{H}\to \mathbb{C}: f\text{ is linear and }\|f\|<\infty\}H∗=B(H,C)={f:H→C:f is linear and ∥f∥<∞}

You can exchange the C\mathbb{C}C with any other field you are interested in.

The Riesz lemma

For each f∈H∗f\in \mathscr{H}^*f∈H∗, there exists a unique vf∈Hv_f\in \mathscr{H}vf​∈H such that f(u)=⟨u,vf⟩f(u)=\langle u,v_f\ranglef(u)=⟨u,vf​⟩ for all u∈Hu\in \mathscr{H}u∈H. And ∥f∥=∥vf∥\|f\|=\|v_f\|∥f∥=∥vf​∥.

[Proof ignored here]

Definition of bounded sesqilinear form

A bounded sesqilinear form on H\mathscr{H}H is a function B:H×H→CB: \mathscr{H}\times \mathscr{H}\to \mathbb{C}B:H×H→C satisfying

  1. B(u,av+bw)=aB(u,v)+bB(u,w)B(u,av+bw)=aB(u,v)+bB(u,w)B(u,av+bw)=aB(u,v)+bB(u,w) for all u,v,w∈Hu,v,w\in \mathscr{H}u,v,w∈H and a,b∈Ca,b\in \mathbb{C}a,b∈C.
  2. B(av+bw,u)=a‾B(v,u)+b‾B(w,u)B(av+bw,u)=\overline{a}B(v,u)+\overline{b}B(w,u)B(av+bw,u)=aB(v,u)+bB(w,u) for all u,v,w∈Hu,v,w\in \mathscr{H}u,v,w∈H and a,b∈Ca,b\in \mathbb{C}a,b∈C.
  3. ∣B(u,v)∣≤C∥u∥∥v∥|B(u,v)|\leq C\|u\|\|v\|∣B(u,v)∣≤C∥u∥∥v∥ for all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H and some constant C>0C>0C>0.

There exists a unique bounded linear operator A∈B(H)A\in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})A∈B(H) such that B(u,v)=⟨Au,v⟩B(u,v)=\langle Au,v\rangleB(u,v)=⟨Au,v⟩ for all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H. The norm of AAA is the smallest constant CCC such that ∣B(u,v)∣≤C∥u∥∥v∥|B(u,v)|\leq C\|u\|\|v\|∣B(u,v)∣≤C∥u∥∥v∥ for all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H.

[Proof ignored here]

The adjoint of a bounded operator

Let A∈B(H)A\in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})A∈B(H). And bounded sesqilinear form B:H×H→CB: \mathscr{H}\times \mathscr{H}\to \mathbb{C}B:H×H→C such that B(u,v)=⟨u,Av⟩B(u,v)=\langle u,Av\rangleB(u,v)=⟨u,Av⟩ for all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H. Then there exists a unique bounded linear operator A∗∈B(H)A^*\in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})A∗∈B(H) such that B(u,v)=⟨A∗u,v⟩B(u,v)=\langle A^*u,v\rangleB(u,v)=⟨A∗u,v⟩ for all u,v∈Hu,v\in \mathscr{H}u,v∈H.

[Proof ignored here]

And ∥A∗∥=∥A∥\|A^*\|=\|A\|∥A∗∥=∥A∥.

Additional properties of bounded operators:

Let A,B∈B(H)A,B\in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})A,B∈B(H) and a,b∈Ca,b\in \mathbb{C}a,b∈C. Then

  1. (aA+bB)∗=a‾A∗+b‾B∗(aA+bB)^*=\overline{a}A^*+\overline{b}B^*(aA+bB)∗=aA∗+bB∗.
  2. (AB)∗=B∗A∗(AB)^*=B^*A^*(AB)∗=B∗A∗.
  3. (A∗)∗=A(A^*)^*=A(A∗)∗=A.
  4. ∥A∗∥=∥A∥\|A^*\|=\|A\|∥A∗∥=∥A∥.
  5. ∥A∗A∥=∥A∥2\|A^*A\|=\|A\|^2∥A∗A∥=∥A∥2.

Definition of self-adjoint operator

An operator A∈B(H)A\in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})A∈B(H) is self-adjoint if A∗=AA^*=AA∗=A.

Definition of normal operator

An operator N∈B(H)N\in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})N∈B(H) is normal if NN∗=N∗NNN^*=N^*NNN∗=N∗N.

Definition of unitary operator

An operator U∈B(H)U\in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})U∈B(H) is unitary if U∗U=UU∗=IU^*U=UU^*=IU∗U=UU∗=I.

where III is the identity operator on H\mathscr{H}H.

Definition of orthogonal projection

An operator P∈B(H)P\in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{H})P∈B(H) is an orthogonal projection if P∗=PP^*=PP∗=P and P2=PP^2=PP2=P.

Tensor product of (infinite-dimensional) Hilbert spaces

Definition of tensor product

Let H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ be two Hilbert spaces. u1∈H1u_1\in \mathscr{H}_1u1​∈H1​ and u2∈H2u_2\in \mathscr{H}_2u2​∈H2​. Then u1⊗u2u_1\otimes u_2u1​⊗u2​ is an conjugate bilinear functional on H1×H2\mathscr{H}_1\times \mathscr{H}_2H1​×H2​.

(u1⊗u2)(v1,v2)=⟨u1,v1⟩H1⟨u2,v2⟩H2(u_1\otimes u_2)(v_1,v_2)=\langle u_1,v_1\rangle_{\mathscr{H}_1}\langle u_2,v_2\rangle_{\mathscr{H}_2}(u1​⊗u2​)(v1​,v2​)=⟨u1​,v1​⟩H1​​⟨u2​,v2​⟩H2​​

Let V\mathscr{V}V be the set of all finite lienar combination of such conjugate bilinear functionals. We define the inner product on V\mathscr{V}V by

⟨u⊗v,u′⊗v′⟩=⟨u,u′⟩H1⟨v,v′⟩H2\langle u\otimes v,u'\otimes v'\rangle=\langle u,u'\rangle_{\mathscr{H}_1}\langle v,v'\rangle_{\mathscr{H}_2}⟨u⊗v,u′⊗v′⟩=⟨u,u′⟩H1​​⟨v,v′⟩H2​​

The infinite-dimensional tensor product of H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​ is the completion (extension of those bilinear functionals to make the set closed) of V\mathscr{V}V with respect to the norm induced by the inner product.

Denoted by H1⊗H2\mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2H1​⊗H2​.

The orthonormal basis of H1⊗H2\mathscr{H}_1\otimes \mathscr{H}_2H1​⊗H2​ is {ui⊗vj:i=1,2,⋯ ,j=1,2,⋯ }\{u_i\otimes v_j:i=1,2,\cdots,j=1,2,\cdots\}{ui​⊗vj​:i=1,2,⋯,j=1,2,⋯}. where uiu_iui​ is the orthonormal basis of H1\mathscr{H}_1H1​ and vjv_jvj​ is the orthonormal basis of H2\mathscr{H}_2H2​.

Fock space

Definition of Fock space

Let H⊗n\mathscr{H}^{\otimes n}H⊗n be the nnn-fold tensor product of H\mathscr{H}H.

Set H⊗0=C\mathscr{H}^{\otimes 0}=\mathbb{C}H⊗0=C.

The Fock space of H\mathscr{H}H is the direct sum of all H⊗n\mathscr{H}^{\otimes n}H⊗n.

F(H)=⨁n=0∞H⊗n\mathscr{F}(\mathscr{H})=\bigoplus_{n=0}^\infty \mathscr{H}^{\otimes n}F(H)=n=0⨁∞​H⊗n

For example, if H=L2(R,λ)\mathscr{H}=L^2(\mathbb{R},\lambda)H=L2(R,λ), then an element in F(H)\mathscr{F}(\mathscr{H})F(H) is a sequence of functions ψ=(ψ0,ψ1(x1),ψ2(x1,x2),⋯ )\psi=(\psi_0,\psi_1(x_1),\psi_2(x_1,x_2),\cdots)ψ=(ψ0​,ψ1​(x1​),ψ2​(x1​,x2​),⋯) such that ∣ψ0∣2+∑n=1∞∫∣ψn(x1,⋯ ,xn)∣2dx1⋯dxn<∞|\psi_0|^2+\sum_{n=1}^\infty \int|\psi_n(x_1,\cdots,x_n)|^2dx_1\cdots dx_n<\infty∣ψ0​∣2+∑n=1∞​∫∣ψn​(x1​,⋯,xn​)∣2dx1​⋯dxn​<∞.
